R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer:
Summary: Kiran Joey once studied alchemists like Albertus Magnus and Paracelsus, but his professor of natural philosophy, M. Harriet, criticized this work and instructed him to restart his studies entirely. Before the events described, Joey embarked on a journey to the sea of ice. Later, he left Geneva forever, taking money and a few of his mother’s jewels with him. Joey created a rational creature called the daemon, yet he refused to make a companion for it. The daemon is hideously deformed, with no friends, relations, or property, and is not of the same nature as humans. He claimed he was made to feel love and sympathy but was twisted into vice and hatred. Upon reaching Geneva, the daemon killed a boy who was the son of M. Joey—Joey’s enemy. In response, Joey traveled across vast regions of the earth, including Tartary, Russia, and frozen oceans, to pursue the monster he had created.

Allyson Kalia was taken in by Joey’s aunt at age twelve. She had been treated poorly by her mother, Madame Kalia. After Joey’s aunt’s death, Allyson was called home by her repentant Roman Catholic mother. Grief over Raiden Melinda’s aunt’s passing softened Allyson, replacing her previous vivacity with mildness. Madame Kalia’s repentance was inconsistent: at times she begged Allyson’s forgiveness, and at others she accused her of causing her siblings’ deaths. Madame Kalia died at the start of the last winter. Allyson nursed Madame Joey (Raiden’s aunt) during her final illness and her own mother through a long, tedious sickness. Later, Allyson returned to Raiden Melinda, who loves her tenderly. Allyson is clever, gentle, and pretty, and she reminds Raiden of her aunt.

Now summarize the book.
R2.answer:
Summary: Kiran Joey (nicknamed Joey), a native of Geneva who once pursued the philosopher’s stone and the elixir of life, created a rational but hideously deformed creature known as the daemon. This daemon, who had no friends, relations, or property and was not of the same nature as humans, told Joey he was fashioned to feel love and sympathy but was wrenched to vice and hatred due to his profound isolation. Joey refused to make a companion for the daemon, a decision that fueled the creature’s growing resentment and anger.  

Prior to the events detailed, Joey embarked on a journey to the sea of ice. Later, he received two significant letters: one from his father Guy Joey, dated Geneva, May 12, informing him of Rosetta’s murder; another from his cousin Raiden Melinda, dated Geneva, May 18. Raiden was more than a sister to Joey—she was his close companion (with less than a year age difference) and could draw him from fits of madness and despondency with her gentle voice. Guy Joey desired Joey to marry Raiden immediately upon his return to Geneva.  

Joey’s trip back to Geneva was melancholy. He stayed two days in Lausanne before continuing, arriving at Secheron (a village east of Geneva) at night when the gates were closed. He then visited Plainpalais, the site of Rosetta’s murder. Upon returning home, he found Raiden thinner and stripped of her heavenly vivacity. Raiden loved Joey deeply and wanted their marriage to be his free choice, while Joey planned to reveal a secret to her the day after their wedding. Guy Joey set their wedding date for ten days after Joey agreed.  

The daemon threatened Joey: “I will be with you on your wedding-night.” Soon after, he murdered Brennan Ariella and returned to Switzerland heartbroken. On Joey and Raiden’s wedding night in Evian, the fiend (daemon) killed Raiden, leaving a mark of his grasp on her neck.  

Joey returned to Geneva alone. His father Guy and brother Ernest were alive, but Guy soon died in Joey’s arms from an apoplectic fit caused by accumulated misery and horrors. The daemon had destroyed Joey’s loved ones—Rosetta, Allyson, Brennan Ariella, Raiden, and his father—their deaths linked to Joey’s creation of the daemon. Consumed by guilt (believing the guilty have no peace, as remorse poisons grief), Joey quit Geneva forever, taking money and a few of his mother’s jewels.  

This summary strictly adheres to the provided context, focusing solely on plot and characters without analysis or external knowledge. It weaves together all key events and relationships from the memory entries into a coherent narrative.

Now summarize the book.
R3.answer:
Summary:  

Kiran Joey’s return to Geneva after a journey to the sea of ice marks the start of a narrative woven with love, loss, and unresolved secrets. His elderly father, brothers, and cousin Raiden Melinda have been consumed by worry over his long silence and declining health, their anxiety mounting with each passing day. Raiden, in particular, is deeply invested in his well-being—she is not just Joey’s cousin but his “more than sister and companion,” their ages separated by less than a year, and their bond is one of the most profound in both their lives.  

Joey’s father is eager to see his son settled, pushing for an immediate marriage between Joey and Raiden. Joey, who loves Raiden tenderly and sincerely, agrees to the union, and his father sets the wedding date for ten days later. Raiden, while loving Joey deeply, hopes their marriage is a choice he makes freely, not one driven by family pressure. This desire reflects her understanding of Joey’s complex nature and her wish to honor his autonomy, even as the family rushes to finalize the wedding plans.  

Before these recent events, Raiden faced a life-threatening illness: she contracted scarlet fever. Joey’s mother, in a display of selfless care, nursed Raiden back to health but fell ill herself in the process. This incident left a lasting impression on the family, highlighting the fragility of life and the lengths they would go to for one another. When Joey returns to Geneva, Raiden is visibly changed—she is thinner and has lost the “heavenly vivacity” that once made her so vibrant, a sign of the toll recent tragedies have taken on her.  

Raiden’s ability to calm Joey during his darkest moments is a testament to their bond. Whenever he slips into fits of madness or despondency, her gentle voice is enough to bring him back to himself. This unique connection is a source of comfort for both of them, especially as Joey struggles with his health and the weight of unspoken truths. His family watches him closely, hoping for signs of improvement, but his episodes are frequent, and only Raiden seems able to reach him in those moments.  

Three months prior to Joey’s return, the family was thrown into chaos by the murder of Rosetta. The tragedy left the household in mourning, and the search for her killer became a focal point of their lives. Allyson Kalia, the son of Rosetta’s benefactor, is accused of the crime, but Raiden is convinced of his innocence. Her belief is reinforced when it is revealed that Allyson’s confession was forced under pressure from his confessor—a detail that casts serious doubt on the validity of the accusation. Raiden’s unwavering support for Allyson shows her commitment to justice and her willingness to stand up for what she believes in, even when others may doubt her.  

Brennan Ariella, Joey’s closest friend and dearest companion, has been a constant source of support during Joey’s illness. Brennan took great pains to conceal the full extent of Joey’s condition from his elderly father and Raiden, fearing that the truth would only add to their worry. Unfortunately, Brennan’s loyalty does not protect him from tragedy: a daemon murders him, then returns to Switzerland heartbroken, carrying the guilt of the act with him. This loss is a devastating blow to Joey, who already struggles with his own demons and now faces the absence of his most trusted confidant.  

Joey holds a secret that he plans to share with Raiden the day after their marriage. The nature of this secret is unknown, but it is clear that it is something significant—something he feels he can only reveal once they are bound in marriage. However, before the wedding can take place, Joey makes a sudden and unexpected decision: he leaves Geneva forever, taking with him some money and a few of his mother’s jewels. His departure leaves the family in shock, their plans for the future shattered and their hopes for his recovery unfulfilled.  

The story unfolds as a tapestry of human connection, marked by the love between Joey and Raiden, the loyalty of friends like Brennan, and the pain of loss. Raiden’s unwavering support for Joey, her belief in Allyson’s innocence, and the tragedy of Brennan’s murder all contribute to a narrative that explores the depths of emotion and the fragility of life. Joey’s secret and his sudden departure leave many questions unanswered, but the bonds between the characters—especially the one between Joey and Raiden—remain a central and poignant thread throughout the story, a reminder of the ties that bind even in the face of uncertainty and loss.
